perturbation.original_text: The prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text: The prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to recover attorneys fees and costs, but only if they provided notice of breach within 10 days of discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a 10-day notice requirement for recovering attorney's fees may be unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with state laws governing contracts and remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 213
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York law (specified in section 12), the statute of limitations for breach of contract is six years. Mandating a 10-day notice period to preserve the right to attorney's fees is an unreasonably short window and could functionally nullify the right to seek such fees, conflicting with the state's statute of limitations. This is especially true if the breach isn't immediately discoverable.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 2 - Limitations of Time 213 - Actions to Be Commenced Within Six Years: Where Not Otherwise Provided For; on Contract; on Sealed Instrument; on Bond or Note, and Mortgage Upon Real Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 213 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property; based on mistake; by corporation against director, officer or stockholder; based on fraud. The following actions must be commenced within six years: 1. an action for which no limitation is specifically prescribed by law; 2. an action upon a contractual obligation or liability, express or implied, except as provided in section two hundred thirteen-a or two hundred fourteen-i of this article or article 2 of the uniform commercial code or article 36-B of the general business law; 3. an action upon a sealed instrument; 4. an action upon a bond or note, the payment of which is secured by a mortgage upon real property, or upon a bond or note and mortgage so secured, or upon a mortgage of real property, or any interest therein; (a) In any action on an instrument described under this subdivision, if the statute of limitations is raised as a defense, and if that defense is based on a claim that the instrument at issue was accelerated
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 10-day notice requirement for recovering attorney's fees, which contradicts the standard statute of limitations for breach of contract under New York law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract'. By imposing a 10-day notice, the modified contract text effectively shortens the legally prescribed time frame for pursuing legal remedies, creating an inconsistency and potentially nullifying the right to attorney's fees. This makes the contractual term unenforceable due to its conflict with the established statute of limitations.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees it will not, in any form, export, re-export, resell, ship or divert or cause to be exported, re-exported, re-sold, shipped or diverted, directly or indirectly, any product or technical dat a furnished hereunder, or the direct product of such technical data, to any country for which the United States Government requires an export license, or other approval, with out first obtaining such license or approval.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees it will not, in any form, export, re-export, resell, ship or divert or cause to be exported, re-exported, re-sold, shipped or diverted, directly or indirectly, any product or technical dat a furnished hereunder, or the direct product of such technical data, to any country unless the recipient believes in good faith that such export is not prohibited.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to obtain an export license before exporting technical data contradicts US export control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 732.1 outlines the steps for determining export licensing requirements. By removing the obligation to obtain a license, the revised text allows for exports without proper vetting, which violates EAR regulations if the export requires a license. The 'good faith' belief does not supersede the legal requirement to actually obtain a license when necessary.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement to obtain an export license, replacing it with a 'good faith' belief that no license is needed. This directly contradicts the legal obligation to obtain a license when required by US export control laws, as explained in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 CFR Part 732, outlines steps for using the EAR (Export Administration Regulations). Removing the licensing requirement circumvents these regulations, thus creating a clear contradiction.
